## What is Startories?

Startories is a directory submission service for SaaS and startups. We submit your startup to over 100+ high-authority directories in your industry to maximize exposure. Our service provides you with quality backlinks that can significantly boost your SEO ranking and help your startup stand out in search results. We also provide detailed reports upon completion, detailing all submissions and backlinks. With our automated process, we can save you 40+ hours compared to manual efforts.

results.title: Starter Story Competitors & Alternatives (2026) | Product Hunt
results.content:
In evaluating Starter Story alternatives, we considered how each option delivers learning (curated stories vs multi-voice community vs research reports), the practicality and specificity of takeaways, niche fit for solo makers and micro-SaaS builders, and the level of interactivity and support. We also weighed accessibility factors like pricing/paywalls and whether you can preview enough to judge value, along with content freshness and signal-to-noise quality.

# The Best Starter StoryAlternatives

The best Starter Storyalternatives areIndie Hackers, Failory, Founder Reports, MicroFounder, and Micro SaaS HQ.

Indie Hackers screenshot

Indie Hackers

Indie Hackers

4.9 · [...] Starter Story is well known for its polished, success-oriented founder case studies that make it easy to binge real business journeys and pull out tactical lessons. The alternatives landscape splits into a few distinct camps: community-first platforms like Indie Hackers that emphasize ongoing discussion and feedback loops, newsletter-style interview brands like Founder Reports that lean into depth and “low fluff” execution takeaways, and niche micro-SaaS ecosystems like MicroFounder and Micro SaaS HQ that focus on solo-buildable businesses—either as a curated directory and distribution channel or as research-backed idea reports plus community. There are also counterbalancing resources like Failory that focus on postmortems and failure analysis for founders who learn best from what went

results.title: Best Writing Apps for Authors (2026) — 26 Tools Compared
results.content:
### 2. Storyist

Price: $59 for desktop; $19 for phone. Free trial.  
Platform(s): macOS, iOS  
First released: 2007  
Star rating: ⭐️⭐️⭐️  
Best for: Full serviceStoryist writing appStoryist takes a “focused” approach for authors looking to write and publish their books. Though it has different modes for novelists, screenwriters, and playwrights, the app itself is pretty minimalist — which is great for writing, but not as great for formatting.

Compared to similar apps, it’s certainly a low-frills setup… as reflected by the reasonable price! We’d still suggest trying Studio (above) first if you’re seeking a budget-friendly, all-in-one app, but Storyist is not a bad alternative — more so if you’re also interested in screenwriting.

results.title: I tested 22 writing tools—yeah, almost all of them—so you don ...
results.content:
You can also duplicate entire workspaces so each book or world gets its own space. That helps with immersion—like jumping into a Discord server for a totally different project. You can sort and collapse everything, and the export options are solid.

There’s a paid plan, but most of it is free and works offline. I love the structure, flexibility, and visual design. This one’s a real contender. Rating: 9/10

## Craft [...] ## Sudowrite

Sudowrite is all about AI. That’s the core. It’s built to help you brainstorm, generate content, expand scenes, and more. It’s packed with features. There’s even a canvas where you can drop notes and images. I like that. It lets you visualize your mess a bit.

But the writing experience is not for me. The interface is too busy, too many popups, too many things happening. You can collapse the UI, which helps. But I’m just not someone who can write surrounded by that much distraction.

Still, if you’re someone who wants AI for feedback on your work, it’s worth a look. Rating: 6.5/10

## AutoCrit [...] The design has always been clean, but not especially writer-focused. The export situation is kind of a dealbreaker though—it only lets you export to HTML or PDF, which makes it hard to keep your work flexible and portable.

And at $15/month, it’s pricey for something that isn’t purpose-built for novels or worldbuilding. Rating: 6/10

## Reedsy Studio

Reedsy Studio is amazing for one thing: turning your finished manuscript into something print-ready. I don’t use it to write, but when I’ve got a draft done, I drop it in here and use it to generate an EPUB or a print-ready PDF.
